entries.position_summary: The country views Iran’s nuclear program as a prime challenge to the nonproliferation regime, asserting that Iran is producing highly enriched uranium without a credible civilian justification. It also accuses Iran of disregarding its NPT-mandated comprehensive safeguards obligations.
entries.quote: Despite the successes of the NPT in its 55 years in force, stressors con�nue to challenge the nonprolifera�on regime. Prime among these challenges con�nues to be Iran’s nuclear program Iran con�nues to accelerate its produc�on of highly enriched uranium – a material for which Iran has no credible civilian purpose. No other country in the world is enriching to this level that does not also possess nuclear weapons. Moreover, Iran has disregarded its obliga�ons under its NPT- mandated comprehensive safeguards agreement.

entries.position_summary: The country underscores the indispensability and independence of IAEA safeguards and urges the three remaining NPT States Parties without Comprehensive Safeguards Agreements to conclude them by the next Review Conference. It also advocates for the universalization of the Additional Protocol.
entries.quote: The IAEA’s safeguards work is indispensable to this effort, and its technical exper�se and independence are key to our collec�ve security. The assurances provided by the impar�al professionals of the IAEA give us all confidence in the NPT’s efficacy. Only three NPT States Par�es remain without Comprehensive Safeguards Agreements, and we call on these countries to enter into such agreements by the Review Conference next year as a strong affirma�on of commitment to the NPT. We are hopeful that the universaliza�on of the Addi�onal Protocol will not be far behind. We view

entries.position_summary: The United States calls for universal adoption of comprehensive safeguards agreements and Additional Protocols, with modified Small Quantities Protocols where applicable, and aims to solidify CSA+AP as the de facto standard by the 2026 Review Conference. It highlights recent progress, including three new CSAs and 141 States Parties with an AP.
entries.quote: Chair, The United States remains committed to achieving universality of comprehensive safeguards agreements (CSAs). Since last year’s Review Conference, three new CSAs entered into force, bringing us ever closer to universal adherence. In addition, 141 States Parties now have an Additional Protocol (AP). Let’s finish the job to make it universal in this review cycle. This would be a fitting achievement for the 2026 Review Conference; it would extend recognition of the combination of a CSA and an Additional Protocol, and where applicable a modified Small Quantities Protocol, as the de facto standard for achieving the

entries.position_summary: The United States warns that Iran is escalating its nuclear program by producing highly enriched uranium and failing to fully cooperate with the IAEA. It argues that Iran is not meeting its safeguards obligations, preventing the Agency from assuring the program is exclusively peaceful.
entries.quote: The United States is profoundly concerned by Tehran’s sustained path of nuclear escalation. Iran is the only country without nuclear weapons that is producing highly enriched uranium. For five years, Iran has refused to fully cooperate with the IAEA and abide by its safeguards commitments; due to this intransigence, the Agency remains unable to provide critical assurances that the program is exclusively peaceful.

entries.position_summary: The statement condemns Iran for accelerating production and accumulation of highly enriched uranium without a credible civilian purpose, noting this level of enrichment is atypical for non-nuclear-weapon states. It further alleges Iran is disregarding its NPT-mandated comprehensive safeguards obligations.
entries.quote: Iran has accelerated its production and accumulation of highly enriched uranium – a material for which Iran has no credible civilian purpose. No other country in the world is enriching to this level that does not also possess nuclear weapons. Moreover, Iran has disregarded its obligations under its NPT-mandated comprehensive safeguards agreement.

entries.position_summary: The statement criticizes Iran for failing to cooperate with the IAEA on undeclared nuclear material, not implementing its Additional Protocol and modified Code 3.1, and expanding enrichment to 60% with advanced centrifuges. It argues these actions lack a credible civilian purpose and urges collective pressure for Iran to comply with its safeguards obligations.
entries.quote: Equally concerning are Iran’s failures to provide the IAEA with technically credible clarifications regarding undeclared locations where the IAEA detected the presence of nuclear material. Iran is not implementing its Additional Protocol and modified Code 3.1 of its safeguards subsidiary arrangements, both of which remain in force. Iran is also expanding its uranium enrichment infrastructure, deploying advanced centrifuges and producing HEU up to 60%, none of which have a credible civilian purpose. We must all insist that Iran

entries.position_summary: The country strongly supports the IAEA’s independent safeguards system and promotes the CSA plus AP as the de facto verification standard under the NPT. It urges universalization of CSAs by the 2026 Review Conference and seeks rapid universalization of the Additional Protocol thereafter.
entries.quote: The NPT – and our collective security – depend on effective IAEA safeguards to verify that nuclear material is not diverted and that states do not pursue undeclared nuclear activities. We all owe a debt of gratitude to the IAEA for its outstanding work, technical excellence, and independence in providing credible assurances to that effect. Through it, the combination of a Comprehensive Safeguards Agreement (CSA) and an Additional Protocol (AP) represents a de facto standard for NPT verification. Universality of CSAs is finally within reach, with only four NPT States Parties having yet to bring one into force. Let’s make the final push for the 2026 Review Conference and let us see to it that universalization of the AP is not far behind.

entries.position_summary: The statement asserts that Iran has not fully addressed IAEA inquiries regarding indications of possible undeclared nuclear material and activities, signaling concern over safeguards compliance. It implicitly urges Iran to cooperate fully with the IAEA to resolve these issues.
entries.quote: And Iran has not yet fully answered questions from the IAEA about indications of possible undeclared nuclear material and activities on its territory.

entries.position_summary: They affirm that the AUKUS transfer of conventionally armed, nuclear-powered submarines to Australia will meet the highest nonproliferation standards and be implemented consistently with the NPT. They emphasize ongoing transparency and engagement with the IAEA, noting the Director General’s positive assessment, and invite participation in a side event on naval nuclear propulsion.
entries.quote: Regarding the AUKUS partnership, the leaders of the United States, United Kingdom, and Australia have made clear that the provision of conventionally armed, nuclear-powered submarines to Australia will be carried out in a manner that sets the highest nonproliferation standard and strengthens the global nonproliferation regime. The AUKUS partners continue to routinely engage with the IAEA and note that IAEA Director General Grossi expressed satisfaction with our transparency and engagement to date. Given the wide interest expressed here, we hope states parties will join our side event today on naval nuclear propulsion and the NPT in Room M2.

entries.position_summary: The United States condemns the DPRK’s unlawful nuclear and missile programs and its threatening rhetoric, viewing them as a serious threat to international peace and the nonproliferation regime. It seeks complete, verifiable denuclearization of the Korean Peninsula through sustained diplomacy and insists on the DPRK’s return to the NPT and IAEA safeguards.
entries.quote: Chair, The DPRK’s unlawful and continuing nuclear and ballistic missile programs constitute a serious and direct threat to international peace and security and to the global nonproliferation regime. The DPRK’s threatening and irresponsible rhetoric has also intensified, including by characterizing some of its missile launches and other military activities as “trial runs” for the use of tactical nuclear weapons. Our goal remains the complete and verifiable denuclearization of the Korean Peninsula. In that regard, the United States is committed to serious, calibrated, and sustained diplomacy with the DPRK, and we will continue to insist on its return to the NPT and IAEA safeguards.

entries.position_summary: The United States commits significant voluntary funding and technical assistance to enable safe and secure access to peaceful nuclear energy, including hundreds of programs on safeguards, nuclear security, and export controls. It reports providing over a quarter-billion dollars in extra-budgetary support to IAEA safeguards since 2015 and expresses a strong commitment to expanding peaceful uses.
entries.quote: In each of these areas, the United States devotes significant financial resources, technology, training and policy assistance on a voluntary basis to assure the fullest, secure and safe access to peaceful nuclear energy and technology. This includes hundreds of support programs conducted worldwide relating to nuclear safeguards, nuclear security, and export controls, as well as more than a quarter of a billion dollars of extrabudgetary IAEA safeguards support since the 2015 Review Conference. The United States is deeply committed to improving access to peaceful uses, and we look forward to discussing this further in our Cluster III statement.

entries.position_summary: The country asserts Syria violated its NPT safeguards by constructing an undeclared plutonium production reactor, criticizes Syria’s lack of substantive cooperation with the IAEA on outstanding safeguards concerns, and urges immediate Syrian cooperation to allow the Agency to verify the peaceful nature of its program.
entries.quote: Concerning Syria, we reiterate that its construction of an undeclared plutonium production reactor at constitutes non-compliance with its NPT safeguards obligations. Syria’s refusal to engage substantively with the IAEA – including on outstanding safeguards questions related to possible undeclared nuclear material or fuel cycle activities in Syria – prevents the Agency from providing assurance that Syria’s nuclear program is exclusively peaceful. Here too, the burden rests with the Syrian leadership to cooperate with the IAEA without further delay.

entries.position_summary: The United States asserts that Iran is failing to meet its IAEA safeguards obligations, including by not cooperating on outstanding issues related to potential undeclared nuclear material and activities. It criticizes Iran’s termination of modified Code 3.1 and suspension of the Additional Protocol, arguing these steps undermine transparency and prevent the IAEA from assuring the peaceful nature of Iran’s program.
entries.quote: The United States remains deeply concerned that, to date, Iran has failed to provide the cooperation required by its safeguards obligations under the Treaty. The burden is on Iran to resolve outstanding safeguards issues. It should do this by providing credible information necessary to clarify IAEA questions regarding potential undeclared nuclear material and activities. Iran’s unilateral decision to terminate implementation of modified Code 3.1 is contrary to its legal obligations, reducing international confidence that all nuclear facilities under construction in Iran are declared. Iran’s decision to end implementation of its Additional Protocol is also deeply troubling. Because of Iran’s unsatisfactory cooperation, the IAEA remains unable to provide assurances regarding the

entries.position_summary: The country argues that Iran’s production of 60% HEU and use of advanced centrifuges lack a credible civilian purpose, heighten proliferation risks, and should be replaced by confidence-building steps, including resolving IAEA safeguards issues. It states it remains committed to diplomacy and a return to full JCPOA implementation, asserting that Iran blocked a swift return by demanding closure of IAEA investigations.
entries.quote: Further, Iran’s production of highly enriched uranium and deployment of advanced centrifuges amplify these legitimate concerns. Iran’s production of HEU up to 60% has no credible civilian purpose. If Iran’s purpose was genuinely peaceful, we should expect it to take actions that build international confidence and de-escalate tensions, rather than engage in nuclear provocations that pose grave proliferation risks. I would note that the United States remains committed to diplomacy. It was Iran that rejected a swift return to full implementation of the JCPOA almost a year ago, demanding the closure of the IAEA’s open safeguards investigations as part of the agreement. As we’ve made clear, the power to resolve these issues is in Iran’s hands alone.

entries.position_summary: The country urges action to prevent abuse of Article X and to adopt principles that deter NPT withdrawals. It argues that withdrawal undermines universality, does not erase responsibility for pre-withdrawal noncompliance, and does not diminish obligations regarding the use or misuse of peaceful nuclear cooperation.
entries.quote: Chair, It is long past time that States Parties address the challenge of abuse of the NPT’s withdrawal right in Article X. Since the DPRK’s announced withdrawal in 2003, four Review Conferences have been unable to reach consensus on ways to discourage similar action by others in the future. NPT States Parties can make a start by acknowledging several basic principles: First, withdrawal from the NPT by definition sets back the goal of universality. Second, it must be recognized that a withdrawing state remains responsible under international law for any unresolved noncompliance prior to withdrawal. And third, a States Party’s withdrawal in no way minimizes its obligations regarding the use or misuse the fruits of peaceful nuclear cooperation.

entries.position_summary: The country urges full compliance with NPT safeguards and advocates making the IAEA Additional Protocol the global standard. It links responsible nuclear supply to adherence to these enhanced safeguards and calls for condemning violations.
entries.quote: Strengthening the NPT also requires that we address nonproliferation concerns openly, and thoroughly. Looking forward, our priority must be to condemn violations where they occur, insist on full compliance with NPT nonproliferation safeguards, and lift standards wherever possible, such as by making the Additional Protocol the standard for IAEA safeguards and for responsible nuclear supply.

entries.position_summary: The United States supports international cooperation on small modular reactors through capacity building and infrastructure assistance, integrating safeguards and security by design. It also backs the IAEA’s Milestones Approach to ensure new nuclear power programs meet the highest standards of safety, security, and safeguards.
entries.quote: In this regard, I note that the United States supports a range of bilateral and regional assistance to countries seeking to cooperate with the United States on small modular reactor technology, including through capacity building programs, infrastructure assistance, and integrating safeguards and security into new reactor designs. These activities are supplemental to our support for IAEA activities, including the Agency’s Milestones Approach, which enables a sound development process for a national nuclear power program that prioritizes the highest standards of safety, security, and safeguards.

entries.position_summary: The country calls for using the NPT review process to advance nuclear restraint through renewed arms control and concrete risk-reduction measures. It insists on full compliance with IAEA safeguards and supports expanding peaceful nuclear uses globally.
entries.quote: At a time of rising instability and risk, let’s use this review process to advance policies and practices of nuclear restraint, including action on arms control and risk reduction, insisting on full compliance with IAEA safeguards, and facilitating an expansion of peaceful uses on a global scale.

entries.position_summary: The country asserts that Russia has violated Director General Grossi’s seven pillars and five principles at the Zaporizhzhya Nuclear Power Plant, creating serious safety and security risks. It further contends that Russia is obstructing full, timely, and unfettered IAEA access to ZNPP, impeding assessment of the plant and implementation of Ukraine’s safeguards agreement.
entries.quote: Director General Grossi’s seven indispensable pillars of nuclear safety and security. They have also contravened his five principles for averting “a catastrophic incident” at the Zaporizhzhya plant. Russia’s actions also disrupt the IAEA’s efforts to secure full, timely, and unfettered access to all areas of ZNPP so that they can assess the safety and security of the plant and implement Ukraine’s safeguards agreement.

entries.position_summary: The delegation condemns Syria’s safeguards non-compliance, the DPRK’s unchecked nuclear and ballistic advances, and Russia’s seizure of Ukraine’s Zaporizhzhia NPP, urging accountability for actions undermining the nonproliferation regime. It criticizes Russia and the PRC for expanding and diversifying their nuclear arsenals while showing little interest in arms control, and warns about destabilizing coordination among Russia, the PRC, the DPRK, and Iran.
entries.quote: agreement, Syria’s non-compliance with its comprehensive safeguards agreement, the DPRK’s unchecked advances of its nuclear and ballistic weapons programs, and Russia’s seizure of Ukraine’s Zaporizhzhia Nuclear Power Plant. Furthermore, Russia and the PRC are expanding and diversifying their nuclear weapons arsenals — showing little or no interest in arms control. Russia, the PRC, and the DPRK, together with Iran, are increasingly cooperating and coordinating with each other in ways that run counter to peace and stability and exacerbate regional tensions. We will have more to say on these topics in our statement on regional issues; however, my delegation wishes to stress that part of keeping the nonproliferation regime strong is holding those whose actions contradict it accountable.

entries.position_summary: The United States urges Syria’s interim authorities to fully cooperate with the IAEA to resolve outstanding questions about undeclared nuclear activities, welcoming recent IAEA engagements and seeking a full resolution. This emphasizes compliance with IAEA safeguards and transparency.
entries.quote: With the departure of Bashar al-Assad, Syria now has an opportunity to turn the page and come clean with the international community about the undeclared nuclear activities conducted by the Assad Regime. We urge Syria’s interim authorities to cooperate fully with the IAEA to resolve all outstanding issues. The United States welcomes the IAEA’s recent engagements with Syria on this matter and hopes that they lead to a full and complete resolution of this issue.

entries.position_summary: Calls on Syria’s interim authorities to fully cooperate with the IAEA to resolve outstanding safeguards non-compliance issues.
entries.quote: Finally, we urge Syria’s interim authori�es to cooperate fully with the IAEA to resolve all outstanding non-compliance issues.

entries.position_summary: The country asserts Syria is in non-compliance with NPT Article III safeguards due to an undeclared reactor and demands full transparency and cooperation with the IAEA. It urges all parties to press Syria to provide access and information so the IAEA can verify the program is exclusively peaceful.
entries.quote: Chair, we again reiterate that Syria’s construction of an undeclared plutonium production reactor at Dair Alzour constitutes non-compliance with its NPT Article III safeguards obligations. It remains the responsibility of Syria to provide full transparency, and we again call on all parties to demand that Syria cooperate with the IAEA without further delay. We hope that Syria’s recent engagement with the IAEA Director General is a first step toward providing the IAEA with the access and information it needs to determine that Syria’s nuclear program is exclusively peaceful.

entries.position_summary: The country supports holding states accountable for fulfilling IAEA safeguards obligations under Article III. It also aligns with the joint statement on the DPRK, emphasizing that the DPRK’s nuclear program remains an active concern within the NPT.
entries.quote: We also welcomed the chair’s first draft recommendation to hold states accountable for their safeguards obligations. This is an essential element of Article III of the NPT and a measure of the overall health of the nonproliferation regime. And we note that 82 States Parties – including the United States – signed onto France and the Republic of Korea’s joint statement on the DPRK’s nuclear program, demonstrating that the DPRK’s destabilizing actions are not a closed issue in the NPT.

entries.position_summary: The statement highlights Iran’s failure to fully cooperate with IAEA safeguards as a key challenge to the NPT, signaling concern over Iran’s compliance. It underscores that such non-cooperation threatens international security and stability and implies a need for Iran to meet its safeguards obligations.
entries.quote: Notwithstanding its many contributions, the NPT continues to face challenges that, if left unaddressed, will pose an increasing threat to international security and stability. These challenges include Iran’s failure to fully cooperate with the IAEA as required by its safeguards

entries.position_summary: The country criticizes Iran for expanding its nuclear program without credible civilian justification, failing to cooperate with the IAEA to clarify its activities, and making provocative statements about nuclear weapons capability.
entries.quote: • Iran is expanding its nuclear program in ways that have no credible civilian justification, failing to provide the IAEA with the necessary cooperation to prove otherwise, and engaging in loose talk about nuclear weapons capability.

entries.position_summary: The country commits to placing all civilian nuclear facilities under IAEA safeguards and annually reports its civil plutonium holdings, with plans to also report civil HEU holdings. This reflects support for robust safeguards and greater transparency to prevent diversion of civilian fissile material to weapons.
entries.quote: To help provide confidence that fissile material in civil use is not diverted to weapons, we make all our civilian nuclear facilities available for IAEA safeguards and we report annually to the IAEA our civil plutonium holdings; we are also looking at taking steps to report our civil holdings of highly-enriched uranium.

entries.position_summary: The country advocates institutionalizing UNODA as the permanent secretariat for the NPT review process. It also supports formalizing the IAEA’s supporting role—particularly on non-proliferation and peaceful uses—by staffing the secretariat for those issue areas.
entries.quote: • Formalizing the United Nations role by establishing the Office for Disarmament Affairs as the permanent secretariat for the Non-Proliferation Treaty review process rather than deciding separately for each Non-Proliferation Treaty review cycle. Such a decision could also formalize the supporting role of the International Atomic Energy Agency in staffing the secretariat for issues of non-proliferation and the peaceful uses of nuclear technology.

entries.position_summary: The United States highlights transparency by publishing historical production data for plutonium and highly enriched uranium for weapons. It has declared hundreds of tons of this material excess and subjected their storage and disposition to IAEA verification.
entries.quote: With respect to existing fissile material, we have made public historical U.S. production of plutonium and highly enriched uranium for nuclear weapons, and we have declared hundreds of tons of such material excess, in many cases with IAEA verification of its storage and disposition.

entries.position_summary: The United States argues that robust nuclear export controls, consistent with Article III, are essential to nonproliferation and actually facilitate legitimate nuclear trade in line with NPT objectives. It highlights ongoing U.S. cooperation to update NSG Guidelines and Control Lists to address evolving technologies and proliferation risks.
entries.quote: Nuclear export controls, including those required under Article III, are another essential nonproliferation measure, necessary to ensure that nuclear-related transfers do not inadvertently contribute to the development of nuclear weapons. I wish to emphasize that nuclear export controls do not impede legitimate nuclear trade; in fact, they enable it and ensure consistency with the NPT’s purposes. The United States has long been a leader in this area, and continues to work with partners to update NSG Guidelines and Control Lists so that they remain effective in the face of evolving trade and technological developments and proliferation concerns.

entries.position_summary: The United States views the DPRK’s missile and WMD programs as significant security threats and calls for the complete denuclearization of North Korea. It urges the DPRK to return to the NPT and to compliance with IAEA safeguards.
entries.quote: Similarly, the DPRK’s ballis�c missile and weapons of mass destruc�on programs pose significant security threats. The United States calls for the complete denucleariza�on of North Korea and its return to the NPT and IAEA

entries.position_summary: The United States emphasizes partnering with the IAEA and other States to expand peaceful nuclear technologies and energy worldwide while upholding stringent nonproliferation, safety, and security standards. It reaffirms the IAEA’s essential role in facilitating peaceful uses and preventing proliferation and commits to advancing domestic and international nuclear energy deployment under these high standards.
entries.quote: The United States partners with the IAEA and other States Parties to advance innovative breakthroughs in nuclear technologies and applications, while maintaining the highest standards of safety, security, and nonproliferation. We are applying our experience and expertise in a wide range of sectors, including providing abundant and affordable nuclear energy solutions and installing state-of-the-art technology at hospitals and research facilities around the world. We reaffirm the essential role of the IAEA in expanding access to peaceful uses while preventing nuclear proliferation. And we reiterate our commitment to unleashing a golden age of American energy, and to empowering partner countries worldwide to deploy nuclear energy – again, under the highest standards of nuclear security, safety, and nonproliferation.

entries.position_summary: The country supports complete denuclearization of the Korean Peninsula and is ready for practical dialogue to secure the DPRK’s return to the NPT and full compliance with IAEA safeguards. It endorses France’s joint statement highlighting the DPRK’s threat to the global nonproliferation regime and urges others to join. This implies support for NPT universality and opposition to NPT withdrawal.
entries.quote: Our goal remains the complete denuclearization of the Korean Peninsula, and we stand ready to pursue calibrated, practical dialogue that results in the DPRK’s return to, and full compliance with, the NPT and IAEA safeguards. And in this regard, we fully endorse France’s joint statement addressing the DPRK’s threat to the global nuclear nonproliferation regime and urge all parties to do the same.

entries.position_summary: The statement credits the NPT with containing proliferation, establishing robust IAEA safeguards, and advancing disarmament through stockpile reductions and arms control. It also praises the spread of nuclear-weapon-free zones and the benefits delivered by peaceful nuclear applications.
entries.quote: Since it entered into force, we have contained nuclear proliferation; developed robust international safeguards to monitor peaceful nuclear programs; made significant steps towards disarmament through stockpile reductions and other arms control measures; seen entire regions adopt nuclear-weapon-free zones; and, through the peaceful uses of nuclear energy, science, and technology, improved the lives of millions.

entries.position_summary: The country underscores the IAEA’s critical role, supporting its verification activities in Iran and its efforts to maintain nuclear safety and security in Ukraine.
entries.quote: The IAEA is playing a critical role to address immediate challenges, whether by providing for verification in Iran or maintaining safety and security in Ukraine.

entries.position_summary: The country supports the IAEA Additional Protocol as essential to achieving the Treaty’s safeguards objectives and advocates its universal adoption. It further argues the AP should be the standard/condition for nuclear supply by exporters.
entries.quote: safeguards objectives of the Treaty. The AP should also be a standard for nuclear supply.

entries.position_summary: The country signals intent to engage on arms control, nuclear risk reduction, and effective verification, prioritizes full compliance with IAEA safeguards, and supports expanding access to peaceful uses of nuclear technology ahead of the next Review Conference.
entries.quote: Ahead of next year’s Review Conference, there is much to be done. Over the next year, we look forward to engaging on ideas for arms control, reducing nuclear risk, and effective verification; on ensuring full compliance with IAEA safeguards; and on expanding access to peaceful uses. As Secretary of State Rubio has repeatedly said, “peace is not a noun, it is a verb.” So, let’s get to work.

entries.position_summary: The country underscores that robust nuclear security, alongside IAEA safeguards, is essential to prevent theft, trafficking, and misuse of nuclear materials and technologies. It calls for the highest standards of nuclear safety and security as peaceful nuclear applications and emerging technologies grow. It supports the IAEA’s work to uphold safety and security in Ukraine and condemns actions that jeopardize these efforts.
entries.quote: In addi�on to safeguards, nuclear security is cri�cally important to preven�ng prolifera�on. Ensuring nuclear facili�es are protected, and the materials and technologies therein are secure, prevents poten�al the�, trafficking, and misuse, including use to inflict significant harm. To that end, we must con�nue to priori�ze the highest standards of nuclear safety and security as peaceful uses of nuclear technology expand and countries pursue new and emerging technologies. We also reaffirm our support for the IAEA’s efforts to uphold nuclear safety and security in Ukraine and condemn ac�ons that jeopardize these efforts.

entries.position_summary: Emphasizes that benefits under the NPT are tied to compliance with Article III as verified by IAEA safeguards, and expresses strong support and appreciation for the IAEA’s work under difficult and risky conditions.
entries.quote: Of course, these benefits are linked directly to compliance with Article III of the Treaty, as verified through IAEA safeguards. I want to thank the IAEA for its exceptional work in this area, which its staff continues to carry out in increasingly challenging circumstances, and even sometimes at great personal risk.

entries.position_summary: The delegation asserts that Syria’s construction of an undeclared plutonium production reactor at Dair Alzour constitutes non-compliance with NPT Article III and its required IAEA safeguards agreement. They reiterate this finding to underscore the need for accountability under the safeguards regime.
entries.quote: Chair, we again reiterate that Syria’s construction of an undeclared plutonium production reactor at Dair Alzour constitutes non-compliance with NPT Article III and its NPT-required safeguards agreement. The only SENSITIVE BUT UNCLASSIFIED

entries.position_summary: The country asserts that Iran’s nuclear program is peaceful and that all Iranian nuclear material is under IAEA safeguards in line with NPT Article III. It implies recognition of Iran’s compliance within the Iran-related regional proliferation context.
entries.quote: peaceful nature of Iran’s nuclear program, or that Iran has placed all nuclear material under safeguards as required by NPT Article III.

entries.position_summary: Supports an immediate global moratorium on producing fissile material for nuclear weapons pending conclusion of an FMCT, and calls for transparency in civilian production of weapons-usable materials, likely through strengthened safeguards and reporting. The aim is to curb weapons-usable material accumulation and build confidence while negotiations proceed.
entries.quote: Pending that negotiation, it is time for all states to declare moratoria on the production of fissile material for use in nuclear weapons and other nuclear explosive devices and to provide transparency into their civil production of weapons usable material.

entries.position_summary: The United States praises the NPT’s success and urges States Parties to use the PrepCom to advance the Treaty through arms control, reducing risks of nuclear conflict, ensuring full compliance with IAEA safeguards, and expanding access to peaceful uses of nuclear technology.
entries.quote: Statement by the United States to the NPT Preparatory Committee Statement by Deputy Assistant Secretary of State Gonzalo Suarez, U.S. Department of State, Bureau of International Security and Nonproliferation New York, NY May 9, 2025 Chair, colleagues, In our opening statement last week, the United States praised the remarkable success of this Treaty over the past fifty-five years and reaffirmed that all States Parties continue to benefit greatly from it. We urged all States Parties to use this PrepCom to develop positive ideas that advance the NPT, whether on arms control, reducing the risk of nuclear conflict, ensuring full compliance with IAEA safeguards, or expanding access to peaceful uses.

entries.position_summary: The United States (with France and the UK) emphasizes transparency and responsible practices, declaring excess fissile material and placing it and all civilian nuclear facilities under IAEA safeguards while annually reporting civil plutonium holdings. It frames these steps as risk-reduction measures that prevent miscalculation and support progress on disarmament commitments under the NPT. The statement contrasts this approach with what it describes as the opacity of China and Russia.
entries.quote: On Steps Eight and Ten, the United States has also declared hundreds of tons of fissile material in excess to nuclear weapons needs and has made much of it available for IAEA safeguards during storage and disposition. In stark contrast to the PRC, the United States makes all our civilian nuclear facilities available for IAEA safeguards and reports annually to the IAEA our civil plutonium holdings. 2000 Steps Nine and Twelve; 2010 Actions Nineteen through Twenty-One: Other Steps for NWS, Transparency, and Reporting: On Steps Nine and Twelve, the P3 commitment to transparency is striking in comparison to the opacity shown by the PRC and Russia. The Working Paper submitted to the 2022 RevCon by the United States, France, and the UK on “Principles and responsible practices for Nuclear Weapon States” represents our shared commitment to transparency and to acting responsibly to reduce nuclear risks, prevent unintended miscalculation and escalation, and support progress on our disarmament commitments. This commitment is also reflected in our NPT national report, which lays out in greater detail the actions I am describing, as

entries.position_summary: The statement argues that Russia’s war against Ukraine endangers Ukrainian nuclear facilities, threatening safety, security, and safeguards, which could erode public trust in nuclear technology and undermine peaceful applications. It warns that such damage would harm global development and climate efforts and negatively impact the NPT, with responsibility attributed to Russia.
entries.quote: Global efforts to promote and support the growth of nuclear power with the highest standards of safety, security, and safeguards is, however, put at risk because of Russia’s war against Ukraine. Any damage to Ukrainian nuclear reactors would feed into public distrust of nuclear technology in general and challenge public acceptance of even the non-power applications of nuclear science and technology in areas like food safety, health, and water resource management. Damage to Ukrainian facilities would damage global development, damage efforts to address climate change, and damage the NPT – and that would be on Russia.

entries.position_summary: The country asserts that resolving Syria’s noncompliance requires granting the IAEA full access to requested sites, information, materials, and personnel to address outstanding safeguards questions.
entries.quote: path to remedying Syria’s noncompliance is for the IAEA to gain access to all requested sites, information, materials, and persons necessary to resolve outstanding safeguards questions.

entries.position_summary: The country urges NPT Parties to strengthen cooperation on peaceful nuclear uses, conditioned by robust safeguards. It supports bilateral obligations that embed such safeguards as a way to protect shared nonproliferation interests.
entries.quote: We encourage NPT Parties to consider actions that reinforce these elements, in particular in their cooperation on peaceful uses of nuclear energy and associated safeguards-related conditions. These bilateral obligations reinforce and protect the shared interests of all NPT Parties.

entries.position_summary: The country praises the U.S.–IAEA–Russia Trilateral Initiative for developing technical approaches with wider applicability for future disarmament verification, including for excess fissile material. It also applauds ongoing work by the IPNDV and the Quad, signaling strong support for strengthening verification mechanisms in cooperation with the IAEA.
entries.quote: Steps Eight and Ten; 2010 Actions Sixteen and Seventeen: Trilateral Initiative and Securing Excess Fissile Material: The important achievements of the Trilateral Initiative between the U.S., IAEA, and Russia in 2002 identified valuable technical approaches that could have broader applicability for future verification efforts. Moreover, we applaud the important work being done by the IPNDV and Quad on disarmament verification.

entries.position_summary: The United States affirms the inalienable right to peaceful nuclear energy consistent with NPT Articles I and II, emphasizing nuclear power’s role in meeting global energy needs and positioning itself as a leading supplier. It stresses that NPT-facilitated cooperation should be contingent on adherence to the highest nonproliferation standards to ensure safety and security, implying robust safeguards and supply conditions.
entries.quote: The United States acknowledges the inalienable right to research, production and use of nuclear energy for peaceful purposes in conformity with NPT obligations under Articles I and II and we continue to emphasize that nuclear energy will significantly contribute to the world’s energy needs, with the United States ready to be the preeminent supplier. The NPT facilitates peaceful nuclear cooperation, and conforming with the highest nonproliferation standards will ensure this cooperation is safe and secure.

entries.position_summary: The speaker contends that, unlike the United States, another state has failed to report its civil plutonium holdings since 2017 and is expanding fissile material production with advanced reactors outside IAEA safeguards. This underscores the speaker’s call for greater transparency in fissile material reporting and for applying IAEA safeguards to relevant facilities.
entries.quote: Nor is it the United States that has declined to report on its civil plutonium holdings since 2017 and is building out its capacity to produce fissile material using advanced nuclear reactors that remain outside of IAEA safeguards.
